text: The Roman philosopher Seneca wrote to his friend Lucilius: 'Withdraw into yourself as much as you can; associate with those who will make a better man of you.' He was warning against the diffuse sociability of Rome — the endless dinner parties and forum gossip — that left a man knowing many people and being known by none. The warning maps almost precisely onto the architecture of modern social feeds. Breadth of acquaintance has never been easier to accumulate; the discipline Seneca commended — of choosing depth over reach, of giving one's full attention to the few — has never been harder to practice.
question: What parallel does the passage draw between Seneca's warning and modern social feeds?

options.text: Both argue that solitude is superior to any form of social engagement.
Both warn against diffuse sociability that produces broad acquaintance but precludes deep mutual knowing.
Both suggest that meaningful relationships require formal settings and ritual.
Both identify urban density as the primary cause of social fragmentation.
